title: Revisiting Machina Arcana 2nd Edition
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=69_RI4iZxuQ

Daniel (@dungeondive) returns to **Machina Arcana** six months after his initial review, and frames the revisit as a *cure for new-game fatigue*. Two recent buys had soured him — Planet Apocalypse he writes off bluntly (he is openly trying to offload his copy), and Legends of Novus he bounced off thanks to a glaring map misprint he is genuinely surprised the Kickstarter preview wave missed. He needed a game he already knew was good. Machina Arcana volunteered.

The session this time is *Horror in the Ice*, scenario one, played with Belinda Warren the bruiser and McKenzie Wallen the crafter. The standout half-hour is a textbook demonstration of why the monster design works: a *Judithian* that turns ethereal whenever it's the only thing on the board, a *Thrall* that exists solely to draw horror events, and a *Bhupathi* doing hit-and-run nibbles — until Belinda sets up a single-trap-lever play that wipes the lot in one go. Then a *Ghast* spawns. The Ghast cannot be targeted by active abilities. The Ghast teleports between action spaces, destroying them. The rest of the game becomes a literal foot-race against an unkillable saboteur.

Daniel calls out openly that with a tighter rulebook on the upcoming expansion, this game could push into his top ten.

----

*New-game fatigue* is a real and underdiscussed thing. Do you have a comfort-game you turn to when nothing new is landing — and what makes a comfort-game work in that role rather than just being a *good* game?
